title: How Interest Rate Changes Affect Credit Card Spending
summary: Using supervisory data covering nearly 80 percent of US credit card accounts active during 2016-2025, Federal Reserve Bank of Boston researchers find that a 1 percentage point increase in credit card APR reduces credit card spending by approximately 8.7 percent in the following month. The effect is concentrated among revolvers (borrowers carrying month-to-month balances), who reduce spending by about 15 percent per 1pp APR increase, while transactors who pay balances in full show no significant response. Lower-credit-score borrowers also exhibit roughly double the sensitivity of higher-credit-score borrowers to interest rate changes.
key_points: A 1 percentage point increase in credit card APR reduces credit card spending by 8.7 percent in the following month, averaging roughly $74 per month in inflation-adjusted terms.

Revolvers (accounts carrying month-to-month balances) reduce spending by about 15 percent per 1pp APR increase, while transactors (those paying balances in full) show no statistically significant response.
Lower-credit-score accounts (below median) reduce spending by about 18 percent per 1pp APR increase, compared to minimal spending change for higher-credit-score accounts.

Credit card APRs are variable, moving with the prime rate, which equals the federal funds rate plus 3 percentage points, with the Fed's policy changes flowing through to credit card rates typically within one month.
The research uses a regression kink design exploiting the typical 29.99 percent APR ceiling in credit card contracts to identify causal effects of rate changes.
Outstanding balances decline by about 4 percent on average per 1pp APR increase, indicating consumers both spend less and reduce debt when borrowing costs rise.
why_it_matters: This research quantifies the transmission mechanism from Fed rate decisions to consumer spending through the credit card channel, which accounted for $5.83 trillion (about 20 percent of all consumer spending) in 2022. Understanding that lower-income and revolver households are most sensitive to rate changes has direct implications for how monetary policy affects economic activity and inequality. For rate path forecasting, this suggests tighter policy has amplified effects on consumer-dependent sectors and high-revolver populations. The finding that transactors are unaffected by rate changes is important for understanding which households actually bear the cost of Fed policy.
macro_context: The paper demonstrates a direct, quantifiable channel through which Fed rate decisions affect consumer spending. Given credit cards represent 20% of consumer spending and the response is highly non-linear across borrower types, this suggests monetary policy transmission is unevenly distributed. The 8.7% spending reduction per 1pp rate increase (15% for revolvers) indicates meaningful multiplier effects from rate hikes on consumer-driven GDP components. For lower-credit-score borrowers, this dynamic may be compounded by the higher margins they already face (19-20pp above prime vs. 11-12pp for high-credit borrowers), making their effective cost of credit particularly sensitive to Fed action.

issues_found: Source body is truncated at end, cutting off the final paragraph about higher-credit-score accounts and balance declines. However, all key quantitative findings (8.7%, 15%, 18%, $74, 4%) are fully present and verifiable.

The working paper citation (Bräuning and Stavins 2025) is referenced in source text as methodology documentation but the working paper itself is not provided—this is not an issue as the article stands independently.

human_review.notes: Source is highly relevant for macro catalysts but truncated. Recommend retrieving full working paper for complete verification of all quantitative findings. The publication date of March 25, 2026 is recent and current relative to TODAY of March 31, 2026.

notes: Research paper from Federal Reserve Bank of Boston analyzing the effect of interest rate changes on credit card spending using Federal Reserve supervisory data. Uses regression kink design exploiting APR ceilings to establish causality. Strong empirical foundation with 80% coverage of US credit card accounts. Relevant for understanding monetary policy transmission to consumer spending. Publication is current (within one week of processing date). Source body truncated at end; some findings about higher-credit-score accounts partially visible. Lower-credit-score accounts show 18% spending reduction per 1pp APR increase, while higher-credit-score accounts show spending barely changes but balances decline. Full verification of complete findings requires the associated working paper.
